findings.title: permissions-generator silently drops shell_blacklist and tier3_blocking when materializing harness permission files
findings.hypothesis: src/permissions-generator.js#resolveDerivedSets (line 66-85) only collects shell_patterns and aioson_commands from non-tier3 tiers, and #buildClaudeSettings (line 125-140) emits only `permissions.allow` entries. The tool-level `shell_blacklist` declared in .aioson/config/autonomy-protocol.json (e.g., `['rm -rf /', 'curl * | sh', '*>*']` for the claude and codex tools) is never read by the generator. tier3_blocking patterns (`git push *`, `npm publish *`, `rm -rf *`, `curl * | sh`) are explicitly skipped from the allow list (line 74) but never emitted as deny rules either. Net effect: the protocol's declared deny policy never reaches the harness configuration. SF-project-15's sandbox shell-gate enforces blacklists for callers that pass opts.policy to executeInSandbox, but Bash commands the harness runs directly (via Claude Bash() permissions) bypass that gate. Users reading autonomy-protocol.json reasonably expect 'rm -rf /' to be blocked at every layer; in practice, only the sandbox layer enforces it.
preconditions: .aioson/config/autonomy-protocol.json declares shell_blacklist or tier3_blocking entries
aioson permissions:generate (or its caller) runs to materialize .claude/settings.json, .codex/permissions.json, .gemini/permissions.toml, .opencode/permissions.yaml
Harness (Claude/Codex) reads the generated permission file at session start
reproduction_steps: Inspect autonomy-protocol.json: 'tools.claude.shell_blacklist' = ['rm -rf /', 'curl * | sh', '*>*']; 'tiers.tier3_blocking.shell_patterns' = ['git push *', 'rm -rf *', 'npm publish *', 'curl * | sh', 'curl * | bash']
Run aioson permissions:generate (or equivalent) in a throwaway clone, then inspect .claude/settings.json — confirm `permissions.allow` is populated but no `permissions.deny`
grep -n 'deny' src/permissions-generator.js — zero hits
Verify that src/autonomy-policy.js#isCommandAllowed still checks shell_blacklist (intact from SF-project-15) — meaning sandbox-level enforcement is fine; only harness-level is missing
evidence: src/permissions-generator.js:66-85 resolveDerivedSets — only reads tier.shell_patterns + tier.aioson_commands; skips tier3 entirely (line 74) without emitting deny
src/permissions-generator.js:125-140 buildClaudeSettings — returns `{ permissions: { allow: [...] } }` with no `deny` key
src/permissions-generator.js:142-150 buildCodexPermissions — emits `shell_allowed` + `aioson_allowed`; no `shell_denied`/`aioson_denied`
src/permissions-generator.js:158-173 buildGeminiToml — same pattern, allow-only
src/permissions-generator.js:180-199 buildOpencodeYaml — same pattern, allow-only
.aioson/config/autonomy-protocol.json lines 67-69 declare tier3_blocking shell_patterns; lines 84, 94 declare tool-level shell_blacklist
findings.impact: User-visible security policy diverges from harness-enforced behavior. An operator reviewing autonomy-protocol.json and seeing `tier3_blocking: ['rm -rf *', 'npm publish *', 'curl * | sh']` reasonably believes those commands are blocked at the harness level. They are not. If a malicious dependency, indirect-injection, or compromised prompt causes the agent to emit `rm -rf $HOME`, Claude's Bash() gate consults only the allow list — and because `rm -rf` is not in allow, Claude will prompt the operator (this is the actual safety net). The medium rating reflects: (a) the safety net remains intact via Claude's default-deny-on-unmatched, but (b) any operator who set allow='*' or 'Bash(rm:*)' loses both layers of defense without realizing it because the protocol's deny intent is silently dropped.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend resolveToolSets/resolveDerivedSets to return a third set `denyPatterns` collected from (a) tool.shell_blacklist and (b) protocol.tiers.tier3_blocking.shell_patterns. Update buildClaudeSettings to emit `permissions.deny` with `Bash(...)` rules derived from those patterns. Mirror in buildCodexPermissions (add `shell_denied`), buildGeminiToml (`shell_denied = [...]`), buildOpencodeYaml (`shell_denied: [...]`). Add regression tests under tests/permissions-generator.test.js asserting that deny entries are emitted and that tier3 shell_patterns reach the deny set even when the tool has no derived_from_tiers tier3 entry.
findings.dev_session_note: Fixed 2026-05-15: src/permissions-generator.js#resolveDerivedSets and resolveLegacySets now also expose denyShellPatterns + denyAiosonCommands; resolveToolSets unions tier3_blocking patterns + tool.shell_blacklist into the deny set regardless of derived_from_tiers. buildClaudeSettings emits permissions.deny (Bash(...) rules); buildCodexPermissions adds shell_denied + aioson_denied; buildGeminiToml/buildOpencodeYaml emit shell_denied/aioson_denied blocks (always present for schema stability, empty when nothing to deny). mergeClaudeSettings preserves operator deny entries as well as allow. 8 new regression tests in tests/permissions-generator.test.js covering each output format + the end-to-end .claude/settings.json deny emission. tests/permissions-generator.test.js 21/21 PASS.
findings.qa_decision_note: Verified 2026-05-15 by @qa: status=fixed confirmed. Physically inspected src/permissions-generator.js: line 79-92 (denyShellSet built from tier3.shell_patterns + tier3.aioson_commands), line 117-120 (resolveToolSets unions tool.shell_blacklist into deny set), line 146-175 (buildClaudeSettings emits permissions.deny only when non-empty), line 177-186 (buildCodexPermissions emits shell_denied + aioson_denied always for schema stability), line 195-222 (buildGeminiToml deny blocks), line 227-258 (buildOpencodeYaml deny blocks). Critical implementation detail confirmed: tier3 deny patterns flow into the deny set REGARDLESS of whether the tool listed tier3_blocking in derived_from_tiers — this matches the suggested_fix intent (declared deny policy reaches the harness even when the tool config omits tier3 from its derivation list). End-to-end test asserts .claude/settings.json contains Bash(git push:*), Bash(rm -rf:*), and Bash(aioson cloud:publish:*:*) in permissions.deny. permissions-generator.test.js 21/21 PASS. SF-15 sandbox-level shell_blacklist gate (round 5 fix) remains intact as the second enforcement layer — defense in depth across sandbox + harness. Gate D decision: fixed.

findings.title: mergeClaudeSettings persistently preserves any existing allow entries across regeneration
findings.hypothesis: src/permissions-generator.js#mergeClaudeSettings (line 211-230) reads the current .claude/settings.json, unions its `permissions.allow` with the freshly-generated allow list, and writes the result back. The intent is to preserve operator-added allow rules between regenerations, but the same code also preserves entries that were injected by a prior compromise (e.g., a Bash(rm:*) entry an attacker wrote once via any path-write vector). There is no diff/warn signal alerting the operator when the existing file contains entries the generator did not produce. Combined with SF-24 (no deny rules emitted), a one-shot injection into .claude/settings.json persists indefinitely and is treated as legitimate operator policy on every subsequent run.
preconditions: .claude/settings.json exists with at least one allow entry the generator did not produce
aioson permissions:generate runs (e.g., post-install, post-protocol-edit, or scheduled regeneration)
reproduction_steps: On a throwaway clone, edit .claude/settings.json and add `Bash(rm:*)` (or any sentinel like `Bash(echo-injected:*)`) to permissions.allow
Run aioson permissions:generate
Re-read .claude/settings.json — confirm the sentinel entry survives
grep -n 'warn\|diff\|unexpected' src/permissions-generator.js — zero hits referencing entry-divergence detection
evidence: src/permissions-generator.js:220-222 — `const existingAllow = ...; const generatedAllow = generated.permissions.allow; const merged = [...new Set([...generatedAllow, ...existingAllow])];`
No warning emitted for entries present in existingAllow but absent from generatedAllow
The generator outputs `merged` directly, so the user never sees a diff log
findings.impact: Defense-in-depth gap. Today the framework targets single-tenant local dev where the operator controls .claude/settings.json directly, so persistence of operator-added entries is desirable UX. The risk surfaces when an attacker has any one-shot write to .claude/settings.json (e.g., via a future vulnerability) — that injected entry then becomes effectively permanent because no audit signal flags it.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (option A) compute the set difference `extra = existingAllow \ generatedAllow` and, when non-empty, log a tier-2 notify or stderr warning naming the unexpected entries so the operator can review; or (option B) make the merge opt-in via a flag and default to strict regeneration. Option A is the least disruptive — preserves current UX while surfacing drift.
findings.dev_session_note: Fixed 2026-05-15 (option A — drift-surface without changing merge UX): src/permissions-generator.js#mergeClaudeSettings now returns { merged, unexpectedAllow } where unexpectedAllow is the set difference existingAllow \ generatedAllow. generatePermissions populates a notices: [{kind: 'unexpected_claude_allow', tool, path, entries, message}] array in its return value when drift is detected. Existing entries are still preserved (UX intact); installer/doctor can surface the notice however they choose. 2 new regression tests in tests/permissions-generator.test.js: one asserts the notice is emitted when injected entries exist, one asserts no notice when existing matches generated.
findings.qa_decision_note: Verified 2026-05-15 by @qa: status=fixed confirmed. Physically inspected src/permissions-generator.js: line 270-303 (mergeClaudeSettings returns {merged, unexpectedAllow}), line 289 (set difference computation), line 325 (notices array initialized), line 349-356 (notice pushed when divergence detected with structured payload kind/tool/path/entries/message). Backward-compat concern checked: installer.js and doctor.js consume generatePermissions result and forward it; they will pass-through the new notices field without breaking on unknown keys. Recommended downstream follow-up (NOT a Gate D blocker): surface the unexpected_claude_allow notice via tier-2 notify or stderr in installer/doctor so operators see the drift signal at the moment it matters most. Filed as residual observation in QA sign-off below. permissions-generator.test.js 21/21 PASS. Gate D decision: fixed.

findings.title: MICRO classification in prd-{slug}.md short-circuits ALL gates including Gate D (security review)
findings.hypothesis: src/handoff-contract.js#checkGateApproval was extended in commit 7d5914e (deyvin-density, 2026-05-11) to short-circuit gate validation when classification is MICRO. The classification value is resolved from prd-{slug}.md frontmatter via parseFrontmatterValue (line 184), with no integrity check, no operator-confirmation requirement, and no constraint that a feature's classification be ≤ the project's classification. A maliciously-influenced (or accidentally mis-classified) prd-{slug}.md setting `classification: MICRO` causes checkGateApproval to return `{ ok: true, reason: 'micro_skips_gate' }` for EVERY gate the contract requires — including Gate D, which is the @qa+@pentester security-review gate. Net effect: a feature can ship through workflow:next from @product directly to @dev to feature:close with verdict=PASS, never passing through any security review, simply by virtue of its PRD declaring MICRO classification.
preconditions: Feature uses workflow:next gate enforcement (default flow)
prd-{slug}.md frontmatter contains `classification: MICRO`
Operator does not manually verify the classification matches feature scope
reproduction_steps: Inspect src/handoff-contract.js:172-191 resolveClassification — reads prd-{slug}.md frontmatter first, falls back to project.context.md
Inspect src/handoff-contract.js:281-289 checkGateApproval — `if (classification === 'MICRO' && slug) return { ok: true, reason: 'micro_skips_gate' };`
Inspect src/handoff-contract.js:301-307 validateHandoffContract — the loop calls checkGateApproval for every contract.gates entry, so MICRO skips Gates A, B, C, AND D

Trace: a prd-{slug}.md with `classification: MICRO` reaches workflow:next → resolveClassification returns 'MICRO' → all gates short-circuit → feature:close --verdict=PASS succeeds without spec, without QA report, without @pentester findings
evidence: src/handoff-contract.js:281-289 the MICRO short-circuit

src/handoff-contract.js:283-285 comment confirms the design intent — '@product → @dev only — they don't pass through @analyst/@architect and therefore don't produce spec-{slug}.md. Skip gate validation entirely for MICRO; the lightweight workflow is the gate.'
tests/handoff-contract-micro.test.js (referenced in 7d5914e commit message) — '7 cases covering MICRO bypass on dev/qa stages, SMALL/MEDIUM unchanged regression'
No code path in src/handoff-contract.js or src/commands/workflow-next.js verifies that classification ≤ project.context.md classification
No operator-confirmation step is required before resolveClassification accepts the prd-derived value
findings.impact: Gate-bypass mechanism that maps directly onto Gate D — the security-review gate. The framework's documented contract is 'MEDIUM features require @qa + @pentester before close'. A feature whose PRD claims MICRO classification can ship without that review even if the actual scope warrants MEDIUM. The vector is realistic because: (a) classification is set by the LLM-driven @product agent at PRD time, (b) the same agent is susceptible to indirect-injection via researchs/ or dossier content (the entire memory_context surface), (c) no operator-side checksum or confirmation gate exists between 'PRD frontmatter' and 'MICRO short-circuit'. Distinct from SF-project-18 (direct workflow.state.json edits) — that vector requires write access to runtime state; this one only requires influence over the @product agent's PRD output.
findings.suggested_fix: Two-layer defense, both small: (1) Enforce 'feature classification ≤ project classification' in resolveClassification — if prd-{slug}.md declares MICRO but project.context.md is MEDIUM, the resolver returns the more-restrictive of the two and logs a warn-level notify naming the conflict. This matches the principle 'feature scope can be narrower than project scope but never wider in terms of process rigor.' (2) Carve Gate D out of the MICRO short-circuit when the project classification is MEDIUM: even for genuine MICRO features inside a MEDIUM project, Gate D should still run because the security baseline is project-wide. Concretely, change the early-return to `if (classification === 'MICRO' && slug && gateLetter !== 'D')` and add a regression test asserting Gate D still runs for a MICRO feature inside a MEDIUM project.
findings.dev_session_note: Fixed 2026-05-15 (option 2 — Gate D carve-out only, not option 1): src/handoff-contract.js gains readProjectClassification(targetDir) that reads project.context.md frontmatter independently of any feature PRD. validateHandoffContract resolves both classification (feature-level, may be PRD-overridden) and projectClassification (always project.context.md). checkGateApproval takes a 5th argument projectClassification and short-circuits MICRO only for process gates A/B/C; Gate D short-circuits only when the *project* classification is also MICRO. Option 1 (force feature ≤ project) was rejected as it would override the deyvin-density-validated design that lets MICRO features in MEDIUM projects use a lightweight workflow for A/B/C — option 2 preserves that UX while restoring the security baseline. Updated 1 pre-existing test (qa-stage-MICRO-passes adds writeProjectContext(MICRO) to match the legitimate scenario the test verifies). 4 new regression tests added: MICRO-in-MEDIUM blocks Gate D, MICRO-in-SMALL blocks Gate D, MICRO-in-MEDIUM passes Gate C (process gate, no security implication), MICRO-in-MEDIUM passes Gate D when spec-{slug}.md carries a recorded QA Sign-off PASS verdict. tests/handoff-contract-micro.test.js 11/11 PASS.
findings.qa_decision_note: Verified 2026-05-15 by @qa: status=fixed confirmed AND design judgment (option 2 only, not option 1) AUDITED AS SOUND. Physically inspected src/handoff-contract.js: line 172-181 (readProjectClassification reads project.context.md independently), line 184-201 (resolveClassification feature-first with project fallback), line 215 (MICRO short-circuit for non-D gates), line 218 (Gate D short-circuit ONLY when project is also MICRO), line 290-295 (validateHandoffContract resolves both classifications), line 304-312 (checkGateApproval receives both as 4th + 5th args). Audited 7 edge cases manually: (1) state.classification='MICRO' override → projectClassification still controls Gate D since readProjectClassification is independent — confirmed; (2) project.context.md missing → readProjectClassification returns null → Gate D falls through to spec check (fail-closed) — confirmed; (3) prd-{slug}.md missing → resolveClassification falls back to projectClassification → consistent behavior — confirmed; (4) prd without classification field → same as (3) — confirmed; (5) MEDIUM feature in MICRO project → classification='MEDIUM' bypasses MICRO short-circuit → full gates apply — confirmed; (6) unknown classification value → falls through to spec check — confirmed; (7) TOCTOU race on project.context.md → readFileSafe returns empty → fail-closed — confirmed. Option 2 alone is sufficient because: (a) security baseline now applies project-wide, (b) fail-closed when project classification unknown, (c) state/PRD overrides do not bypass the baseline (orthogonal layers). Option 1 would have only prevented legitimate MICRO-in-MEDIUM lightweight workflow (a UX cost without security gain given option 2). The pre-existing test update (writeProjectContext(MICRO) added to qa-stage-MICRO-passes) is correct test semantics — the old test was implicitly asserting the exact gate-bypass behavior SF-26 documents, so adding the project context makes the test verify the LEGITIMATE short-circuit scope. handoff-contract-micro.test.js 11/11 PASS + handoff-contract-pentester.test.js + handoff-contract-v2.test.js + handoff-validator.test.js + workflow-next-validator-routing.test.js together = 56/56 PASS (no regression of any handoff-contract behavior). Gate D decision: fixed.

findings.title: memory:trim --archive=<path> had no project-root containment (path traversal / arbitrary overwrite)
findings.hypothesis: --archive was resolved relative to cwd with no boundary check, so a crafted or mistyped path (../.., absolute) could write or overwrite a file outside the project. Tier-2, hook-blocked, human-typed CLI flag — only the human at the terminal can set it, so realistic risk is a foot-gun or a wrapper passing untrusted input.
preconditions: user runs aioson memory:trim with --archive=<path>
reproduction_steps: aioson memory:trim . --keep=3 --archive=../escape.md
(pre-fix) archive written to the parent dir, outside the project
evidence: src/commands/memory-trim.js — archivePath = path.resolve(process.cwd(), options.archive) with no containment
findings.impact: Pre-fix, a malicious/typo path could create or overwrite a file anywhere writable. Low: requires human CLI action; command is AIOSON_RUNTIME_HOOK-blocked so no automation/LLM path reaches it.
findings.suggested_fix: Resolve --archive under the project root and reject absolute/.. escape (archive_path_escape), mirroring memory-reflect-commit containment.
findings.dev_session_note: Fixed 2026-05-29 (commit d4d12d4): --archive resolved under project root via path.resolve(root, ...); rejects resolved!==root && !startsWith(root+sep) → archive_path_escape; i18n added in 4 locales (cli.-prefixed). Regression: tests/memory-trim-security.test.js.
findings.qa_verification: Verified 2026-05-29 by @qa: containment correct; sibling-prefix bypass (/proj-evil vs /proj/) prevented by +path.sep; 4 tests adequate. Residual R-1 (lexical resolve, no symlink) + R-2 (Windows case-sensitivity, fail-safe) accepted as low. PASS.

findings.title: feature:close auto-trim hook bypassed the AIOSON_RUNTIME_HOOK tier-2 guard
findings.hypothesis: The feature:close auto-trim hook called the trim engine directly, not gated by the AIOSON_RUNTIME_HOOK block that memory:trim enforces. If feature:close ran in a hook/automation context, a tier-2 memory mutation would fire where memory:trim would refuse — inconsistent with BR-ALL-01.
preconditions: feature:close --verdict=PASS invoked with AIOSON_RUNTIME_HOOK=1
reproduction_steps: AIOSON_RUNTIME_HOOK=1 aioson feature:close . --feature=x --verdict=PASS
(pre-fix) the trim hook mutates current-state.md inside a hook context
evidence: src/commands/feature-close.js — trim hook skipTrim did not check AIOSON_RUNTIME_HOOK
findings.impact: Pre-fix, a tier-2 memory mutation could execute from automation. Low: feature:close PASS is a deliberate Gate-D human action today; defense-in-depth.
findings.suggested_fix: Skip the auto-trim when process.env.AIOSON_RUNTIME_HOOK === 1.
findings.dev_session_note: Fixed 2026-05-29 (commit d4d12d4): skipTrim now also true when AIOSON_RUNTIME_HOOK===1. Regression: tests/memory-trim-security.test.js.
findings.qa_verification: Verified 2026-05-29 by @qa: guard matches memory:trim hook-block; test confirms hook-context skip. PASS.
